passage: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: The passage is mainly about how the library

options.A: failed because the building was too old
options.B: became useful by changing how villagers viewed education
options.C: was built by retired teachers alone
options.D: was meant only for children who liked novels

answer_text: became useful by changing how villagers viewed education

explanation.question_restate: We are to answer a JAMB-style comprehension question by relying closely on what the passage says, suggests, or implies.
explanation.principle: The main idea is the broad point that ties together the beginning, middle and end of the passage.
steps: The passage starts with doubt about the project and then shows how it gradually became useful.
The details about farmers, children and adults all point to a wider educational value.
The last sentence says the library changed what many people thought education was for.
So the deepest central idea is not the building itself but the way it changed attitudes toward learning.
explanation.final_answer: B. became useful by changing how villagers viewed education
explanation.simple_explanation: The correct option is the one that stays closest to the passage's meaning, tone, logic or evidence. For more guided practice and fuller beginner-friendly explanation, check our "Tutorial section".

passage: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: The writer's main purpose in the passage is to

options.A: mock students who live in hostels
options.B: show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste
options.C: argue that government should never provide water
options.D: prove that only environmental clubs understand science

answer_text: show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste

explanation.question_restate: We are to answer a JAMB-style comprehension question by relying closely on what the passage says, suggests, or implies.
explanation.principle: Writer's purpose asks why the passage was written and what effect it is meant to have on the reader.
steps: The passage begins with a water problem and then introduces a practical proposal.
It describes resistance to the idea and later presents visible benefits from the pilot scheme.
The final sentence reinforces the value of what first seemed like a desperate idea.
So the writer's purpose is to show that sensible conservation can reduce waste and ease scarcity.
explanation.final_answer: B. show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste
explanation.simple_explanation: The correct option is the one that stays closest to the passage's meaning, tone, logic or evidence. For more guided practice and fuller beginner-friendly explanation, check our "Tutorial section".

passage: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.

options.B: Customers always prefer neat buttonholes to fast service
options.C: Family poverty is the only reason apprentices enter tailoring
options.D: Ankara material is the hardest fabric to cut

answer_text: Tailoring requires discipline, and mistakes can become part of learning

explanation.question_restate: We are to answer a JAMB-style comprehension question by relying closely on what the passage says, suggests, or implies.
explanation.principle: A topic sentence expresses the controlling idea around which the whole passage is organized.
steps: The passage begins with Sade's discovery that sewing requires discipline.

Option A combines both the need for discipline and the value of learning through mistakes.
The other options focus too narrowly on single details.
explanation.final_answer: A. Tailoring requires discipline, and mistakes can become part of learning
explanation.simple_explanation: The correct option is the one that stays closest to the passage's meaning, tone, logic or evidence. For more guided practice and fuller beginner-friendly explanation, check our "Tutorial section".

passage: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.

passage: For months, the water tank near the health centre stood like a promise that had forgotten how to speak. It had been installed with much celebration, yet no water flowed from the taps beneath it. Women still walked long distances to the stream, and patients who came early to the clinic often left thirsty. The problem, according to the maintenance officer, was not the tank itself but the absence of a functioning pump and the careless damage done to two connecting pipes during a roadside repair. When a local association raised money for replacement parts and persuaded the council to provide a technician, the tank finally began to serve the neighbourhood. Children no longer carried buckets before school, and the queue at the stream shortened. Nobody described the repair as a miracle. It was simply a lesson in how public applause means little when maintenance is neglected.

passage: The science club at Liberty Secondary School decided to build a small weather station after repeated floods damaged the school garden. At the first meeting, members argued over whether such a project was too ambitious for students who had never handled measuring instruments before. The club patron disagreed. He reminded them that careful observation often begins with simple tools and disciplined record-keeping. With money raised from a book fair, the students bought rain gauges, a thermometer and a wind vane made by a local artisan. They then divided themselves into teams responsible for morning readings, afternoon readings and data recording. The first month was discouraging because several entries were incomplete and one instrument was damaged by rough handling. Instead of abandoning the project, the club reviewed its mistakes, created clearer duty rosters and trained younger members more patiently. By the end of the term, the records were accurate enough for the students to compare rainfall patterns and identify the weeks when flooding was most likely.

passage: When the neighbourhood transformer failed, darkness spread through the street long before families had finished preparing supper. passage: For years, traders in Oke-Ona market judged the time by guesswork, by the length of shadows, or by asking bus drivers passing the edge of the road. That loose habit began to cause quarrels when delivery vans arrived late and some traders insisted they had been kept waiting for hours. To reduce the confusion, the market union installed a large clock above the entrance arch. At first, many people laughed at the idea, saying that a clock could not change dishonesty. Yet after a few weeks, the arguments over delivery time reduced, shopkeepers opened and closed more regularly, and the food sellers near the back lane found that customers trusted their announced lunch hour more than before. The clock did not create perfect honesty, but it gave the market a shared reference point. In places where memory and convenience often compete, a visible standard can quietly improve behaviour.

passage: At first, the reading contest at Sunrise Junior School appeared to reward speed alone. Pupils stood before the class and raced through paragraphs while classmates counted the number of lines completed in one minute. The English teacher soon noticed that many competitors were swallowing punctuation, flattening meaning and finishing passages they clearly did not understand. She therefore changed the rules. Contestants would now lose marks for missing pauses, ignoring expression or misreading key words. The new format disappointed some pupils who had enjoyed winning through breathless speed, but it encouraged others who read more slowly and more thoughtfully. After two weeks, the performances became less noisy and more meaningful. By the final round, even the fastest readers had learnt that fluency without understanding was only a show, not a skill.

passage: After two years of poor harvests, the elders of Umueke invited an agricultural officer to advise them on how to use the abandoned land behind the health post. Some expected the officer to recommend imported seeds and expensive machinery. Instead, she suggested planting a community orchard with hardy fruit trees that could survive irregular rainfall. Her argument was simple: while annual crops failed quickly in uncertain weather, trees, once established, would provide shade, fruit and a small income over time. The plan was not welcomed by everybody. Younger farmers complained that trees required too much patience, and some women worried that the land would become difficult to share fairly. Yet the first set of seedlings was planted, and the village created a register to track who watered which section. A year later, the orchard still looked small, but goats no longer wandered through the area, and the land that had once produced nothing had begun to hold value again.

passage: For years, the notice board outside the assembly hall had been treated as decoration rather than communication. passage: For years, the notice board outside the assembly hall had been treated as decoration rather than communication. Papers were pinned carelessly, old announcements remained months after events had passed, and urgent notices were often hidden behind colourful flyers for entertainment shows. Matters changed when a newly appointed vice-principal asked each department to submit notices through a single office and fixed a rule that outdated items must be removed every Friday. passage: Every morning at six, the local radio station broadcasts a short programme on farming, health and civic information. passage: At the main bus stop in Idare, passengers used to rush toward every arriving vehicle in a tight, noisy crowd. Elderly people were often pushed aside, schoolchildren lost sandals in the scramble, and arguments broke out whenever two passengers claimed the same seat. The transport union's first attempt to fix the problem failed because the painted lines on the ground faded quickly and no one enforced them. A second attempt worked better. Two attendants were assigned to separate routes, call passengers in order and hold back those who tried to jump forward. Complaints did not disappear completely, but the number of quarrels dropped, loading became faster and drivers spent less time honking impatiently. Some passengers grumbled that the new method felt slow, yet many secretly preferred an orderly queue to the old disorder that wasted both time and dignity.

passage: In front of the district court stood an old mango tree under which petition writers, bicycle repairers and newspaper vendors worked every day. To an outsider, the tree looked like an untidy accident in a serious public space. To regular visitors, however, it served as shade, landmark and waiting room. passage: On Monday morning, the senior prefect pinned two notices to the board outside the principal's office. The first announced a debate rehearsal for all registered speakers after school on Wednesday. The second warned final-year students that library identity cards must be renewed before the end of the week. By Tuesday afternoon, several pupils were already telling one another that the rehearsal had been cancelled. The rumour began because someone noticed that the first notice had curled at one corner and assumed it was outdated. When the English teacher discovered the confusion, she read the notice aloud in class and reminded students that poor reading is not the same as missing information. By Wednesday, the rehearsal held as planned, while a few final-year students who had ignored the second notice were refused entry to the library. The incident showed that misunderstanding often grows, not from the absence of information, but from careless attention to it.

passage: When the women's cooperative opened a small bakery beside the roadside clinic, some neighbours predicted that it would close before the year ended. Their doubt was not unreasonable. Earlier attempts at similar businesses had collapsed because records were poorly kept, ingredients were bought on impulse, and credit was given too freely to friends who never paid on time. This time, however, the committee insisted on a different method. Flour, sugar and yeast were measured against weekly plans, every sale was entered in a ledger before sunset, and members took turns checking stock at the end of each day. The bakery's loaves were not larger or sweeter than those sold elsewhere, but customers began to trust that they would be fresh each morning. After six months, the cooperative had not become rich, yet it had earned enough to repair two ovens and start a savings fund for emergencies. The women later said the bakery succeeded not because they had discovered a secret recipe, but because they finally respected routine.

options.D: survived only by giving more credit to friends
